sentence: Subjective:
The patient presented with a history of coughing for approximately three months, initially noticed while working with horses and loading hay, which improved after a cold.
The patient is a previous smoker who quit seven years ago and has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, well-controlled with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
The patient exercises regularly with biking and has a family history of breast cancer but no history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
Review of systems:
The patient denies pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications:
I prescribed methotrexate 7.5mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, afebrile.
Physical exam:

Lungs are clear and equal bilateral, heart has a regular rate and rhythm without clicks, rubs, or murmurs, extremities have pink, warm, and dry skin without edema, and pulses are intact with brisk capillary refill.
Test Results:
The patient had a chest CT that showed a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, smooth in appearance, and no emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, status: confirmed.
Problem list:
Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; Unknown.
Plan:
I ordered a pulmonary function test (PFT) to obtain a baseline for lung function.
I scheduled a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.

I plan to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy, which involves three small incisions on the right chest, under general anesthesia, and will require a chest tube for one day post-procedure.
The patient will be admitted for one night and discharged the following day.
The patient should continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and continue medication therapy for rheumatoid arthritis.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again in a few minutes to review and sign the paperwork for the treatment process.
